summary: A stellar 1967 underground‑FM broadcast where Jerry & Phil spin an eclectic mix of blues, folk and avant‑garde while bantering with Tom Donahue – captured in a clean, downloadable recording.
review: This tape is a rare glimpse into the musical world that shaped the Dead in ’67, featuring Jerry Garcia and Phil Lesh riffing on everything from Charles Ives to Junior Wells, with Tom Donahue’s free‑form FM vibe. Reviewers rave about the intelligent dialogue, the surprising playlist (Golden Road, Cream Puff War, Morning Dew, etc.) and the surprisingly clear audio, noting only occasional link problems. Overall it is praised as both an essential historical document and a pleasant listening experience.

ai_rating.rationale: Over twenty reviews (mostly 4‑5 stars) highlight the show’s unique content and high enjoyment, while noting consistently good audio quality; only a single technical complaint appears, giving the rating a strong positive bias.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Listeners repeatedly mention the download’s clarity and “great” sound; one reviewer reports a broken link but no complaints about hiss, muffling or balance.

standout_songs: Sittin' On Top Of The World
Sad Eyed Lady of the Lowlands
show_quality.setlist_flow: Reviewers describe an eclectic, free‑form sequence that jumps from blues to avant‑garde classical and back, stitched together by lively banter between Jerry, Phil and DJ Tom Donahue, giving the broadcast a natural, conversational flow.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Repeatedly called intelligent and engaged; he offers a tribute to Otis Redding and shares his personal music discoveries.
band_member_comments.Phil: Described as a bit slurred but insightful; famously calls the first Dead LP “a turd” and contributes thoughtful commentary on jazz, the Beatles and other influences.
band_member_comments.Bob: Absent from the session, though he is noted for contributing a rendition of Dylan’s “Maggie's Farm”.
